rulings.Case Title: ALIGN TECHNOLOGY, INC. VS. NICOLE LAMBERT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Default Judgment And Dismiss Action For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, November 7, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANT NICOLE LAMBERT's Motion To Vacate Default Judgment And Dismiss Action For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction Defendant Lambert's motion to vacate the default entered on February 7, 2018 and default judgment entered on June 5, 2018 is GRANTED. The evidence demonstrates that plaintiff failed to serve defendant in accordance with the California Code of Civil Procedure. Defendant's request to dismiss the action based on insufficient minimum contacts is denied without prejudice. Based on the present record, the court cannot determine whether defendant is subject to the alleged forum selection clause and that issue can be raised by a subsequent motion. rulings.Case Title: CHANTAL MARTIN DEL CAMPO VS. GENERAL MOTORS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 7, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF CHANTAL DEL CAMPO'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is GRANTED and DENIED as follows. (1) The parties are ordered to enter into a standard form protective order. If the parties cannot agree to the form of an order, both parties shall submit a proposed protective order by 5 pm today via email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com and shall appear at the hearing. (2) The Court makes the following rulings-The Motion is GRANTED as to RFPs 7, 17, 20, 22, 23, 24, 26, 54, 55, 56, 69. (3) All attorney-client privilege and attorney- work product objections are sustained. Defendant shall provide a privilege log of documents withheld on the basis of privilege. All other objections are overruled. (4) Each RFP is limited in scope to documents related or applicable to the subject vehicle only. (5) Amended responses and responsive documents shall be produced within two weeks of entry of this order and filing of a protective order, whichever is later. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to pcatalanotti@fmglaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GABRIELA LOPEZ VS. LA CASA DE LAS MADRES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, November 7, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT LA CASA DE LAS MADRES' DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant La Casa De Las Madres demurrer to amended complaint is OVERRULED. The court liberally construes the amended complaint pursuant to CCP 452. Plaintiff alleges sufficient facts showing that defendant failed to accommodate plaintiff and ultimately terminated her employment because of her pregnancy. (See Amended Complaint, pars. 18-23.) These facts plead discrimination. The court overrules the demurrer to the harassment claim. Paragraphs 17 and 35 sufficiently allege harassing conduct. A factual issue remains whether defendant's conduct was sufficiently severe, pervasive, or hostile to constitute harassment. The court overrules the demurrer to the failure to prevent claim given this analysis. The court overrules the demurrer to the disability discrimination cause of action. "In the context of disability discrimination, the plaintiff initially has the burden to establish a prima facie case of discrimination. The plaintiff can meet this burden by presenting evidence that demonstrates, even circumstantially or by inference, that he or she (1) suffered from a disability, or was regarded as suffering from a disability; (2) could perform the essential duties of the job with or without reasonable accommodations, and (3) was subjected to an adverse employment action because of the disability or perceived disability." (Sandell v. Taylor-Listug, Inc. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 297, 310.) In this case, plaintiff's pregnancy was the disability (Amended Complaint pars. 14-19), plaintiff could perform the job with an accommodation (Amended Complaint, par. 19) and plaintiff was subject to an adverse employment action (Amended Complaint, pars. 23-24). The court overrules the demurrers to the failure to accommodate and interactive process claims. Defendant misconstrues paragraph 21. The court overrules the demurrer to the wrongful termination cause of action because the FEHA claims are viable. The court overrules the demurrer to the intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action. (See Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 288.) The court overrules the demurrer to the misrepresentation cause of action. Defendant presumptively knows what misrepresentations its agent made to prospective employers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and judgment and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/srb

rulings.Case Title: VERONICA MCCLUSKEY VS. JEFF HENRY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, Alternatively, To Stay Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 1281.4
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, November 7, 2018, Line 15, DEFENDANTs JEFF HENRY, DAVE WILLNER, and SANAZ EBRAHIMI's Motion To Compel Arbitration Or, Alternatively, To Stay Pursuant To Cal. Code Civ. Proc. Section 1281.4 Defendants Jeff Henry, Dave Willner, and Sanaz Ebrahimi's ("Defendants") motion to compel arbitration is GRANTED. In addition, this case is stayed pending the conclusion of the arbitration proceedings. Defendants alleged wrongful acts occurred after Ms. McCluskey had consented to AirBnB's Terms of Service (TOS), version 6, which contains an arbitration clause. By agreeing to submit disputes about the "enforcement, interpretation or validity" of the agreement to binding arbitration, the parties clearly and expressly delegated challenges to the enforceability of the agreement, including challenges based on unconscionability, to the arbitrator. (See Aanderud v. Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 880, 892.) Ms. McCluskey's claim that the TOS as a whole is an illegal contract is also an issue to be decided by the arbitrator. The U.S. Supreme Court in Buckeye Check Cashing, Inc. v. Cardegna (2006) 546 U.S. 440, 446-449 rejected the distinction between voidable contracts and void contracts, and held that challenges to the illegality of a contract as a whole are to be decided by the arbitrator. The Defendants may compel arbitration of the claims against them because the alleged wrongful conduct was committed within the scope of their employment with AirBnB and is related to the TOS. There is sufficient "identity of interest" between signatory AirBnB and the non-signatory Defendants such that the non-signatory Defendants should be permitted to enforce the arbitration provision. Also, the allegations against all parties are inextricably intertwined. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and judgment and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/srb
